text: The surgeon attempts to identify the appendix, which is in a retrocecal position, by mobilizing and rotating the cecum to improve visibility.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter during mobilization, visible with peristalsis, located a few centimeters from the dissection area. Traction is applied, and the incision in the peritoneum allows carbon dioxide to expand the tissue planes, revealing the precise dissection area.

The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery to perform sharp dissection, revealing the ureter's course and creating an avascular plane. The carbon dioxide insufflation aids in opening the dissection plane, allowing the appendix to gradually come into view.

The surgeon uses cautery to incise the lateral attachment of the peritoneum in the retroperitoneal space, revealing the appendix's location. The appendix is positioned near the sub-hepatic region, with its tip stopping just short of the liver.

The surgeon uses cautery to dissect and identify the plane around the appendix, dividing unconventional fascial attachments to visualize the appendix fully. Traction and counter-traction help open the plane, revealing the tubular structure of the appendix. Small fascial attachments tethering the appendix near its base are visible, and the surgeon prepares to incise carefully near the terminal ileum, which joins the cecum at the ileocecal junction.

The surgeon performs further dissection to rotate the cecum and clearly visualize the appendix from the retroperitoneal structures. Small fascial attachments covering the appendix are incised to expose the appendix and mesoappendix, facilitating the appendectomy under clear vision.

The surgeon uses the cecum as a landmark to guide the dissection, gradually removing tissues with monopolar cautery. Thin fascial attachments over the anti-mesenteric border of the appendix are coagulated, revealing the bare appendix structure.

The surgeon identifies and holds thin facial attachments around the appendix, using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to open a window exposing the mesoappendix and appendix. Short bursts of monopolar cautery are applied to dissect remaining attachments, revealing some fat and small vessels, which are carefully managed with sharp dissection.

The surgeon continues the dissection by releasing attachments around the appendix using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection with cautery. The tip of the appendix is grasped and traction is applied to expose it further. The cecum is identified and precautions are taken to avoid injury, ensuring a safe margin for dissection. The appendix is now bare on one side, and the structures are clearly visible.

The surgeon applies traction to reveal the translucent opposite side of the appendix, indicating the absence of major structures. Small, thin attachments are visible, and the mesoappendix begins to appear in the plane with vessels running behind it. The surgeon identifies small vessels and fibrotic strands, using bipolar cautery and ligature to coagulate and divide them, ensuring a clear surgical field.

The surgeon twists the appendix to identify and isolate the mesoappendix from the appendix, aiming to minimize its size for easier removal through a tenon port. The cecum is clearly identified to ensure precise dissection at the appendix base. The terminal small bowel is noted nearby, avoiding cautery to prevent thermal damage. Additional adhesions are removed using an energy source, with small vessels branching from the appendicular branch of the ileocolic artery visible.

The surgeon carefully dissects the anterior and posterior leaves of the peritoneum near the base of the appendix, using energy sources like bipolar cautery to avoid thermal injury. Small attachments are meticulously taken down, creating a clear window for adhesiolysis. The appendix is freed, allowing for the application of an endo loop. The cecum is identified and tissues are pulled away from its wall to prevent inadvertent damage, ensuring safe dissection around the appendix base.

The surgeon identifies the tinea coli on the appendix, noting its lateral, middle, and medial aspects. The base of the appendix is milked to push any fecolith distally. The surgeon holds the appendix with an instrument to find a firm area for placing the endo loop, ensuring the cecum is not inadvertently pulled up during traction.

The surgeon palpates the base of the appendix, identifying a firm area suitable for endo loop application. Two endo loops are applied proximally, spaced five millimeters apart, followed by a third endo loop distally to prevent fecolith spillage. A butadiene-soaked gauze is used for chemical cauterization. The appendix is removed through a ten-millimeter port using claw forceps, ensuring it does not contact the skin.
